text.title: Cognitive Inclusion Design Patterns by Profile
text.summary: Concrete design patterns for four cognitive profiles: general cognitive load reduction, ADHD, autism spectrum, and dyslexia.

rule.statement: Structure all content with a clear visual hierarchy, chunk information into groups of 5 or fewer items, and make navigation predictable and consistent across screens.
rule.rationale: Working memory limits apply to all users; cognitive disabilities lower the threshold, so patterns that reduce load universally provide the highest inclusion return.

list.item: Clear heading hierarchy — users scan by heading, not by reading paragraph by paragraph.
Non-intrusive notifications — user-controllable timing and position.
Error messages — plain language, non-blaming ("The email address looks incomplete" not "Invalid input"), with a clear corrective action.
Time limits — extend or eliminate where possible; always warn before expiry and offer extension without loss of progress.

rule.statement: Provide progress indicators for all multi-step tasks, auto-save on every input, and a bookmark/resume capability for long flows.
rule.rationale: ADHD impairs working memory and sustained attention; losing progress on interruption causes abandonment and distrust.

list.item: Progress indicators: step N of M, percentage complete, or visual stepper.
Auto-save: triggered on every field blur or every 30 seconds — never on page close only.
Reduced-motion option: provided as a settings toggle, not assumed system-wide via prefers-reduced-motion alone.
Focus mode: hides non-essential navigation and ambient content during task completion.

rule.statement: Use literal, unambiguous language throughout — no idioms, metaphors, or sarcasm — and warn users before any screen transition or state change.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous language and unexpected transitions are common sources of anxiety and disorientation for autistic users.

list.item: Predictable interactions: same action always produces same result across the product.
Clear visual boundaries between content regions.
Sensory-friendly color palettes: avoid high saturation or flashing content; offer a low-stimulation theme.
Transition warnings: modal or inline notice before navigating away from a partially-completed task.

rule.statement: Set default line height to at least 1.5, left-align all body text (never justify), and offer a dyslexia-friendly font as an opt-in setting rather than a global default.
rule.rationale: Justified text creates uneven word spacing that disrupts reading flow for dyslexic readers; left-aligned text preserves consistent spacing. Dyslexia fonts (e.g., OpenDyslexic) have weak empirical support as universal defaults but are valued by individual users.

list.item: Letter spacing: offer a setting to increase spacing to at least 0.12em.
High contrast modes: multiple options beyond system dark mode.
Text-to-speech integration: accessible via keyboard, not hidden in menus.
